content.text: Music generation is harder than speech synthesis. Speech has a single dominant signal — the voice — with constrained phonetic structure and known prosodic patterns. Music must simultaneously model
(verse-chorus form, thematic development). A model that sounds locally plausible but globally incoherent will produce music that 'goes nowhere'.
Three distinct paradigms have emerged, each making different tradeoffs between controllability, audio fidelity, and structural coherence.

attrs.alt: Music generation landscape: symbolic MIDI models (Music Transformer, MuseNet, MusicVAE), codec language models (MusicGen pipeline with EnCodec), and latent diffusion models (AudioLDM 2, Stable Audio). MusicGen codebook interleaving diagram, evaluation metrics, and model comparison table.

content.text: — pitch, duration, velocity, timing — rather than raw audio. They produce MIDI files, which are then rendered by a software synthesiser or sampler. The advantage is perfect structure control: you can post-process the output, transpose it, change instruments, or feed it into a DAW.
Music Transformer (Huang et al., 2018) addresses the central problem of long-range musical structure using

. Standard Transformer attention weights depend on the absolute position of each token, which can't express that 'note at t+8 is related to note at t because they are both on beat 1'. Relative attention encodes the distance between tokens, letting the model learn that notes 16 or 32 steps apart (one or two bars) are structurally related.

Music Transformer generates piano performance MIDI that exhibits thematic recapitulation — melodies that return in later sections — which previous models with absolute position embeddings could not reliably do.

MuseNet (OpenAI, 2019) applies GPT-2 to a MIDI token vocabulary encoding pitch, velocity, timing offsets, and instrument identity. Trained on a large MIDI corpus spanning classical, jazz, pop, and folk, it can generate 4-minute compositions for up to 10 simultaneous instruments. Conditioning is achieved by prepending style tokens (composer, genre, instruments) to the generation prompt — a prompting approach that predates instruction-tuning by two years.
MusicGen (Copet et al., Meta, 2023) generates audio directly by modelling sequences of

. EnCodec encodes audio at 75 tokens/second with 4–8 residual vector quantisation (RVQ) codebooks. The main challenge: 4 codebooks per frame means the sequence is 4x longer than a single-stream model, and the codebooks are hierarchically dependent (lower codebooks capture coarse structure, upper codebooks fine detail).

for multi-codebook generation. Rather than cascading separate models (codebook 1 → codebook 2 → …) or generating all codebooks in parallel (which ignores inter-codebook dependencies), MusicGen uses a single Transformer that generates all codebooks in a staggered schedule. At step t the model predicts codebook 1 for frame t, codebook 2 for frame t-1, codebook 3 for frame t-2, and codebook 4 for frame t-3. This introduces a 3-frame latency but lets a single model capture all inter-codebook dependencies in one forward pass.

MusicGen uses a T5 text encoder to produce conditioning embeddings from a text prompt (e.g., 'upbeat jazz with trumpet and drums, 120 BPM'). These embeddings condition the Transformer decoder via cross-attention. Optionally, a melody conditioning signal can be provided: a reference audio clip or hummed melody is encoded as chroma features (12-bin pitch class histogram per frame), which the model uses to guide the harmonic content of the output while allowing the timbre to vary freely.

# pip install audiocraft torch
from audiocraft.models import MusicGen
from audiocraft.data.audio import audio_write

# Load model: 'small' (300M), 'medium' (1.5B), 'large' (3.3B), 'melody'
model = MusicGen.get_pretrained('facebook/musicgen-small')
model.set_generation_params(
    duration=10,         # seconds
    top_k=250,
    top_p=0.0,
    temperature=1.0,
    cfg_coef=3.0,        # classifier-free guidance coefficient
)

# Text-to-music generation
descriptions = [
    "upbeat jazz piano with brushed drums, 120 BPM",
    "ambient orchestral, cinematic, slow build, no drums",
]
wav = model.generate(descriptions)  # (B, 1, T)
for i, desc in enumerate(descriptions):
    audio_write(
        f"music_{i}",
        wav[i].cpu(),
        model.sample_rate,
        strategy="loudness",    # normalise loudness
    )
    print(f"Saved music_{i}.wav ({model.sample_rate} Hz)")

# Melody-conditioned generation
import torchaudio
from audiocraft.models import MusicGen
from audiocraft.data.audio import audio_write
from audiocraft.data.audio_utils import convert_audio

model = MusicGen.get_pretrained('facebook/musicgen-melody')
model.set_generation_params(duration=8)

# Load a melody reference (e.g., a hummed tune or piano lead)
melody_wav, sr = torchaudio.load("melody_reference.wav")
melody_wav = convert_audio(melody_wav, sr, model.sample_rate, model.audio_channels)
melody_wav = melody_wav.unsqueeze(0)   # (1, C, T)

descriptions = ["electronic dance music, heavy bass, energetic"]
wav = model.generate_with_chroma(
    descriptions=descriptions,
    melody_wavs=melody_wav,
    melody_sample_rate=model.sample_rate,
    progress=True,
)
audio_write("melody_conditioned", wav[0].cpu(), model.sample_rate, strategy="loudness")
MusicLM (Agostinelli et al., Google, 2023) uses a three-stage hierarchical model. The
generates coarse semantic tokens from MuLan (Music Language Model, a CLAP-like audio-text embedding model). The
conditions on semantic tokens to generate coarse SoundStream tokens. The
fills in the remaining SoundStream codebooks. Each stage is a separate autoregressive model.
(a sequence of time-stamped text prompts that change the mood across the generated clip). This long-form generation with changing context is a unique capability not yet in open-source alternatives.
Latent Diffusion: AudioLDM 2 and Stable Audio
Diffusion-based models operate in the latent space of a VAE trained on mel-spectrograms.

(Liu et al., 2023) unifies text-to-audio and text-to-music generation: a CLAP model encodes the text prompt into an audio-aligned embedding; a Language Model (GPT-2 variant) generates a coarse latent sequence from the CLAP embedding; a diffusion U-Net then iteratively denoises this latent over 200 steps; a HiFi-GAN vocoder decodes the mel to waveform.

# pip install diffusers transformers torch torchaudio soundfile
from diffusers import AudioLDM2Pipeline
import torch, soundfile as sf

pipe = AudioLDM2Pipeline.from_pretrained(
    "cvssp/audioldm2-music",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

prompt = "lofi hip hop, chill beats, vinyl crackle, 80 BPM"
negative_prompt = "low quality, noise, distorted, harsh"

audio = pipe(
    prompt,
    negative_prompt=negative_prompt,
    num_inference_steps=200,
    audio_length_in_s=10.0,
    num_waveforms_per_prompt=1,
    guidance_scale=3.5,
).audios[0]   # np.ndarray shape (samples,)

sf.write("audioldm2_output.wav", audio, samplerate=16000)
print(f"Generated {len(audio)/16000:.1f} s of audio")
Stable Audio 2 (Stability AI, 2024) pushes output quality further: it generates

audio up to 3 minutes long using a latent diffusion model with a long-context Transformer backbone conditioned on CLAP and T5 text embeddings. A timing conditioner allows specifying the total clip length and the structure of segments (intro, verse, chorus). The architecture is designed to produce full, ready-to-use music tracks rather than short demos.

Both codec LMs and diffusion models use CFG to balance prompt adherence with diversity. During training, the conditioning is randomly dropped (replaced with a null embedding) with some probability. At inference, two forward passes are run — one with the text conditioning and one without:

(typically 3–7 for music) produces output that more faithfully follows the prompt but sacrifices diversity. For codec LMs like MusicGen, the guidance is applied to logits rather than noise predictions.
Computes Frechet distance between VGGish feature distributions of generated and reference audio. Analogous to FID for images. Lower is better. MusicGen-large achieves FAD 4.4 on MusicCaps.
Between PaSST classifier label distributions on generated vs reference audio. Measures tonal and genre accuracy.
Cosine similarity between CLAP embeddings of the text prompt and the generated audio. Measures prompt adherence; analogous to CLIP score for image generation.
Human ratings on a 1–5 scale for naturalness, musicality, and prompt adherence. Ground-truth reference but expensive to collect.

AudioCraft is Meta's open-source framework that bundles MusicGen, AudioGen (text-to-sound-effects), and EnCodec in one package. It provides a consistent API, training code, and evaluation tools. MusicGen-stereo (1.5B) is the recommended model for most production uses.

# Batch generation with continuation (extend existing audio)
from audiocraft.models import MusicGen
from audiocraft.data.audio import audio_write
import torchaudio, torch

model = MusicGen.get_pretrained('facebook/musicgen-stereo-small')
model.set_generation_params(duration=15)

# Continue from an existing audio prompt
existing, sr = torchaudio.load("intro.wav")
existing = existing.unsqueeze(0)         # (1, C, T)

wav = model.generate_continuation(
    prompt=existing,
    prompt_sample_rate=sr,
    descriptions=["continue with orchestral swell, full strings"],
    progress=True,
)
for i, w in enumerate(wav):
    audio_write(f"continuation_{i}", w.cpu(), model.sample_rate)

# Long-form: generate in rolling 30s windows
model.set_generation_params(duration=30)
full_wav = model.generate(["cinematic background, 2 minutes"])
audio_write("long_form", full_wav[0].cpu(), model.sample_rate, strategy="loudness")

Commercial models produce significantly more polished output than current open-source alternatives. Suno v3/v4 and Udio generate full songs with vocals, lyrics, and instrument separation at production quality, using proprietary codec-LM architectures with RLHF fine-tuning for musicality. However, their training data sources are contested in ongoing copyright litigation (RIAA lawsuit, 2024). Practitioners using commercial music generation must review the platform's terms regarding commercial rights and attribution requirements.

EnCodec (Defossez et al., Meta, 2022) is the neural audio codec that underpins MusicGen. It is a convolutional encoder-decoder with residual vector quantisation (RVQ). The encoder compresses 24 kHz audio into a sequence of latent vectors at 75 Hz (128x compression). RVQ then quantises each latent with a cascade of codebooks — each codebook captures the residual left unexplained by prior codebooks:
encodes the coarsest structure: overall pitch, rhythm, and genre. This alone produces intelligible but rough audio.
progressively add timbral detail, instrument separation, and fine frequency content. The full 4-codebook representation reconstructs near-lossless audio.

# EnCodec: encode audio to codec tokens and back
# pip install encodec
import torchaudio
from encodec import EncodecModel
from encodec.utils import convert_audio
import torch

# 24 kHz model with 4 codebooks at 75 tokens/second
model = EncodecModel.encodec_model_24khz()
model.set_target_bandwidth(3.0)   # kbps; controls number of active codebooks

wav, sr = torchaudio.load("music.wav")
wav = convert_audio(wav, sr, model.sample_rate, model.channels).unsqueeze(0)

# Encode to codec tokens
with torch.no_grad():
    encoded = model.encode(wav)  # list of (codes, scale)
codes = encoded[0][0]  # (B, n_codebooks, T_tokens)
print(f"Codec tokens: {codes.shape}")
# e.g., torch.Size([1, 4, 750]) for 10 s audio @ 75 tokens/s

# Decode back to audio
with torch.no_grad():
    decoded = model.decode(encoded)
print(f"Decoded audio: {decoded.shape}")
torchaudio.save("reconstructed.wav", decoded[0].cpu(), model.sample_rate)

A related capability is music source separation: decomposing a mixed track into stems (vocals, drums, bass, other). This is useful for remixing, training data preparation, and evaluation. Demucs (Meta, 2023) is the current open-source standard, using a hybrid time-domain and spectrogram U-Net:

# pip install demucs
# Command-line: demucs -n htdemucs music.mp3
# Python API:
from demucs.pretrained import get_model
from demucs.apply import apply_model
import torchaudio, torch

model = get_model("htdemucs")   # Hybrid Transformer Demucs
model.eval()

wav, sr = torchaudio.load("music.mp3")
wav = wav.unsqueeze(0)   # (1, C, T)

with torch.no_grad():
    sources = apply_model(model, wav, device="cpu")
# sources: (1, 4, C, T) — [drums, bass, other, vocals]

stem_names = ["drums", "bass", "other", "vocals"]
for i, name in enumerate(stem_names):
    torchaudio.save(f"{name}.wav", sources[0, i].cpu(), sr)
    print(f"Saved {name}.wav")
Effective text prompts for music generation follow a consistent structure. MusicGen and AudioLDM 2 respond well to descriptors in this order:
genre → mood → instrumentation → tempo/BPM → production style
. Overly abstract prompts ('happy music') produce generic results; specific technical prompts produce more distinctive output.
"Rhodes piano, fretless bass, soft brushed snare"
Music generation models require massive audio datasets. The legal landscape is contested:
was trained on 20,000 hours of licensed music plus 390,000 instrument-only tracks from ShutterStock/Pond5 — all legally licensed.
uses FreeSound (Creative Commons) and AudioSet (YouTube). Music portion uses MusicCaps annotations.
are alleged to have trained on copyrighted recordings without licence. The RIAA filed suit in June 2024. Output from these models carries commercial use uncertainty pending legal resolution.

In the US, purely AI-generated music (no human creative input) is not copyrightable per USPTO guidance (2023). Output is in the public domain unless a human author can demonstrate sufficient creative contribution.
Music generation requires modelling melody, harmony, rhythm, timbre, and long-range structure simultaneously — significantly harder than speech synthesis.
Three paradigms: symbolic/MIDI (controllable, requires renderer), codec LM (MusicGen, end-to-end audio, fast), diffusion (AudioLDM 2, Stable Audio, high quality, slow).
MusicGen's delay-pattern codebook interleaving generates all RVQ codebooks in a single Transformer without cascaded models, improving speed and quality.
Classifier-free guidance (CFG scale 3–7) controls prompt adherence; too high causes mode collapse and harmonic repetition.
Evaluate with FAD (distribution similarity), KL divergence (genre accuracy), CLAP score (text adherence), and human MOS.
AudioCraft bundles MusicGen + AudioGen + EnCodec under a consistent API; MusicGen-stereo-small (300M) is the practical starting point.
Commercial models (Suno, Udio) outperform open-source in full-song quality but carry copyright uncertainty; check platform terms before commercial use.
